We should be more careful to do things by which we may gain and keep a good reputation than to increase or add to a large fortune.
Divine Providence has ordered it so that some are rich, and others poor, but all are guilty before God; and at the throne of God’s grace the poor are as welcome as the rich.
Faith foresees the evil coming upon sinners, and looks to Jesus Christ as the sure refuge from the storm.
Where there is a fear of God, there will be humility. And much can be enjoyed because of it: spiritual riches, and eternal life at last.
The way of sin is troublesome and dangerous. But the way of duty is safe and easy.

Train children, not in the way they want to go, that is, according to their corrupt hearts, but in the way they should go; in which, if you love them, you would have them go. Every child should be led as soon as possible to knowledge of the Savior.

This shows how important it is for every man to stay out of debt. As to things of this life, there is a difference between the rich and the poor; but let the poor remember, it is the Lord that made the difference.
The power which many abuse will soon fail them.
He who seeks to relieve the wants and miseries of others will be blessed.
Profane scoffers and revilers disturb the peace.
God will be a Friend to a man in whose spirit there is no deceit; this honor belongs to all saints.
God turns the counsels and plans of treacherous men to their own confusion.
The lazy man talks about a lion outside, but does not consider his real danger from the devil, that roaring lion within, and from his own laziness, which destroys him.
The vile sin of licentiousness commonly besots the mind beyond recovery.

Sin is foolishness; it is in the heart. There is an inward inclination to sin: children bring it into the world with them; and it clings close to the soul. We all need to be corrected by our heavenly Father.
We are but stewards, and must distribute what God entrusts to our care, according to his will.

To these words, to this knowledge, the ear must be bowed down, and the heart applied by faith and love. To live a life of delight in God and dependence on him is the foundation of all practical religion. The way to know the certainty of the word of truth is to make conscience of our duty.
He who robs and oppresses the poor does so at his peril. And if men will not appear for them, God will.
Our corrupt hearts have so much tinder in them that it is dangerous to be around those who throw about the sparks of their passion.
Every man ought to be just to himself, and his family; those are not so, who, by folly or other carelessness, waste what they have.

We are taught not to trespass on another man’s right. And it is hard to find a truly industrious man. Such a man will rise. Do you see a man diligent in the business of religion? He is likely to excel. Let us then be diligent in God's work.
